faqs.question: How many MCA seats are available through NIMCET?
faqs.answer: NIMCET offers over 1,000 MCA seats across all participating NITs. The exact seat matrix varies each year based on the participating institutes and their intake capacity.

faqs.question: Is there any age limit to appear for NIMCET?
faqs.answer: No, there is no upper age limit to appear for NIMCET. Any candidate fulfilling the educational eligibility criteria can apply.

faqs.question: Can a B.Tech. graduate apply for NIMCET?
faqs.answer: Yes, B.E./B.Tech. graduates with Mathematics as a subject in their qualifying degree and a minimum of 60% aggregate marks (55% for SC/ST/PwD) are eligible to apply for NIMCET.

faqs.question: What IELTS band score is required for studying abroad?
faqs.answer: Most universities require a minimum overall band score of 6.0–7.0. Top universities (e.g., Oxbridge, Ivy League equivalents) may require 7.0–7.5 or above. Requirements vary by institution and programme, so always check directly with the university.

faqs.question: What is the difference between IELTS Academic and IELTS General Training?
faqs.answer: IELTS Academic is for students applying to undergraduate or postgraduate programmes, or for professional registration (e.g., doctors, nurses). IELTS General Training is for those seeking work experience, training programmes, or immigration to countries like Australia, Canada, New Zealand, and the UK.

faqs.question: How many times can I retake the IELTS test?
faqs.answer: There is no limit on the number of times you can retake IELTS. You can book a new test as soon as you feel prepared, and most test centres allow bookings up to 3 months in advance.

faqs.question: How long are IELTS results valid?
faqs.answer: IELTS Test Report Forms (TRF) are valid for 2 years from the date of the test. After 2 years, organisations may or may not accept them depending on their policy.

faqs.question: How many attempts are allowed for JEE Main?
faqs.answer: Candidates can appear in JEE Main for a maximum of 6 attempts over 3 consecutive years (2 sessions per year). The best score across all attempts is considered for ranking.

faqs.question: Is JEE Main sufficient for IIT admission?
faqs.answer: No. JEE Main qualifies candidates for JEE Advanced, which is the separate entrance exam for IITs. Admission to IITs requires qualifying JEE Advanced and securing an All India Rank (AIR) within the seat matrix.

faqs.question: What is the JEE Main percentile required for NIT admission?
faqs.answer: For top NITs (like NIT Trichy, NIT Warangal, NIT Surathkal), a JEE Main percentile of 98.5+ is generally needed for competitive branches. For other NITs, a percentile of 90+ may suffice depending on the branch and category.

faqs.question: Who should take the BACE exam?
faqs.answer: The BACE is ideal for students completing a biotechnology certificate or AS/BS degree programme, as well as entry-level lab technicians who want an industry-recognised credential to strengthen their CV when applying for positions in pharmaceutical, agricultural biotech, or research laboratory settings.

faqs.question: How long is the BACE credential valid?
faqs.answer: The BACE credential is valid for 3 years from the date of passing. To maintain the credential, holders must either complete approved continuing education credits or retake the examination before the expiry date.

faqs.question: Is the BACE accepted nationally across the USA?
faqs.answer: Yes, the BACE is recognised by biotech, pharmaceutical, and agriscience employers across the United States as a validated benchmark of entry-level biotechnician competency. It is particularly respected among Florida-based programmes, but its recognition is growing nationally.

faqs.question: Is the Communication & Literacy Skills (CLS) test mandatory for all Massachusetts teachers?
faqs.answer: Yes, every candidate seeking an Initial educator licence in Massachusetts must pass both parts of the MTEL Communication & Literacy Skills test (Reading and Writing), regardless of the subject area they plan to teach.

faqs.question: What is the passing score for MTEL tests?
faqs.answer: The passing scaled score for all MTEL tests is 240, on a scale of 100 to 300. Scores are reported as scaled scores, not raw percentages.

faqs.question: How many times can I retake an MTEL test?
faqs.answer: There is no limit on the number of retakes. However, candidates must wait until the official score from their most recent attempt is released before registering for the same test again. A fee is charged for each attempt.

faqs.question: How many IIMs accept CAT scores?
faqs.answer: All 21 IIMs accept CAT scores. Additionally, over 1,200 other MBA colleges and management institutes across India use CAT scores for their admission processes.

faqs.question: What percentile is required to get IIM calls for interviews?
faqs.answer: Top IIMs like IIM Ahmedabad, Bangalore, and Calcutta typically shortlist candidates with 99+ percentile for general category. Other IIMs may shortlist from 90–98 percentile range. However, IIMs also weigh academic profiles, work experience, and diversity, so the overall profile matters significantly.

faqs.question: Is there a restriction on the number of CAT attempts?
faqs.answer: No. There is no upper limit on the number of times you can appear for CAT. Since CAT is held once a year, each calendar year counts as one attempt, and candidates can keep applying until they achieve their desired score.

faqs.question: What is the difference between MCQ and TITA questions in CAT?
faqs.answer: MCQ questions have 4 answer options and carry a penalty of -1 for wrong answers. TITA (Type In The Answer) questions require candidates to type the answer directly — they carry no negative marking, making them relatively safer to attempt.

faqs.question: What is the age limit for RRB NTPC 2026?
faqs.answer: For most RRB NTPC Graduate and Undergraduate posts, the age limit is 18–33 years as of the notified cut-off date. Standard relaxation applies: 5 years for SC/ST, 3 years for OBC (Non-Creamy Layer), and up to 10+ years for PwD candidates depending on category.

faqs.question: Is there negative marking in RRB exams?
faqs.answer: Yes. For all RRB CBT (Computer Based Test) stages, 1/3 of the mark assigned to a question is deducted for each wrong answer. Unattempted questions carry no penalty.

faqs.question: What is the difference between RRB NTPC CBT 1 and CBT 2?
faqs.answer: CBT 1 is a common screening test for all NTPC posts (100 questions, 90 min). Shortlisted candidates then appear for CBT 2, which is post-specific and slightly more detailed (120 questions, 90 min). CBT 2 scores are used for the final merit list.

faqs.question: Which class of students sit the BECE?
faqs.answer: Students in JSS3 (Junior Secondary School 3, equivalent to Grade 9 or Basic 9) sit the BECE as their exit examination from the lower secondary/basic education tier. It is taken after completing three years of junior secondary education.

faqs.question: What grades are considered passing in BECE?
faqs.answer: BECE grades run from A1 (Excellent, highest) to F9 (Fail, lowest). Grades A1 through C6 are generally considered passing grades required for Senior Secondary School (SS1) admission. Grades D7 and E8 are pass-level but may not meet specific school requirements, while F9 is a fail.

faqs.question: How does the BECE differ from WAEC's BECE in Ghana?
faqs.answer: Nigeria's BECE is conducted by NECO (National Examinations Council) for JSS3 students. Ghana's BECE is a separate examination conducted by WAEC for JHS3 students under a different curriculum. They share the same name but are distinct exams administered by different bodies in different countries.

faqs.question: How is the HOSA Nutrition event scored?

faqs.question: What study materials are recommended for HOSA Nutrition?

Recommended references include the USDA Dietary Guidelines 2020-2025, standard college-level nutrition textbooks (such as 'Nutrition: Concepts and Controversies' by Whitney & Rolfes), and the Academy of Nutrition and Dietetics publications.

faqs.question: Is the HOSA Nutrition event available at all competition levels?
faqs.answer: The Nutrition event is available at the State Leadership Conference (SLC) level. Top finishers at SLC may advance to the National Leadership Conference (NLC). Availability at area/regional competitions depends on the state chapter's event schedule.

faqs.question: What is the best way to prepare for the FBLA Intro to Marketing event?
faqs.answer: Review the current FBLA Competitive Events Guidelines thoroughly, then study with an introductory marketing textbook (such as Kotler & Armstrong's 'Principles of Marketing'). Practice with previous FBLA tests and online mock exams, focusing on the 4Ps, consumer behavior, and market research topics.

faqs.question: Can students compete in Introduction to Marketing Concepts at the National Leadership Conference (NLC)?
faqs.answer: Yes. Students who qualify at the State Leadership Conference (SLC) by finishing in a top position may advance to the National Leadership Conference (NLC) to compete nationally. The same objective-test format applies at all levels.

faqs.question: What are the eligibility requirements for the CPPB exam?
faqs.answer: Candidates must be active NIGP members with at least 2 years of public-sector procurement experience (or 1 year with a qualifying degree). All experience must be in a public agency or publicly funded organisation. Candidates must also agree to UPPCC's Code of Ethics and submit a formal application.

faqs.question: What is the passing score for the CPPB?
faqs.answer: Candidates must achieve a scaled score equivalent to 70% or higher to pass the CPPB examination. Scores are reported as scaled scores, and UPPCC does not disclose raw score-to-scaled-score conversion tables.

faqs.question: How does the CPPB differ from the CPPO?
faqs.answer: The CPPB (Certified Professional Public Buyer) is designed for non-managerial procurement professionals, focusing on operational buying activities. The CPPO (Certified Public Procurement Officer) is the higher-level certification targeting procurement managers and officers with more experience and leadership responsibilities. Both are awarded by UPPCC.

faqs.question: Is HOSA Forensic Science an individual or team event?
faqs.answer: HOSA Forensic Science is strictly a team event with exactly two participants per team. Both members collaborate on the Round 2 written case study analysis. Round 1 objective test performance contributes to the overall team score.

faqs.question: What topics should I prioritise for the Round 1 objective test?
faqs.answer: Focus heavily on forensic pathology (cause & manner of death, PMI), toxicology (drug metabolism, BAC calculations), DNA/serology analysis, and crime scene investigation procedures. These areas tend to have the highest question density in HOSA Forensic Science tests.

faqs.question: Where can I find official study materials for HOSA Forensic Science?
faqs.answer: The official HOSA Competitive Events Guidelines (released annually at hosa.org) list the topic areas and recommended references. Common references include forensic science textbooks by Richard Saferstein ('Criminalistics: An Introduction to Forensic Science') and Henry Lee, as well as HOSA's own practice resources.

syllabusOverview.topics: Literary Narrative / Prose Fiction: Character, plot, tone, theme analysis; Social Sciences (history, economics, sociology, political science passages): Main idea, author's purpose, inference; Humanities (art, music, architecture, philosophy passages): Vocabulary in context, comparative analysis; Natural Sciences (biology, chemistry, physics, earth science passages): Detail, inference, generalisation

syllabusOverview.subject: Science Reasoning
syllabusOverview.topics: Data Representation: Interpreting graphs, tables, charts, and figures from scientific experiments; Research Summaries: Evaluating experimental design, hypotheses, procedures, and conclusions; Conflicting Viewpoints: Analysing two or more scientists' differing explanations of the same phenomenon (covers Biology, Chemistry, Physics, Earth/Space Science)

syllabusOverview.subject: Writing (Optional)
syllabusOverview.topics: Persuasive/analytical essay responding to a given societal/educational prompt with three provided perspectives; Evaluated on: Ideas & Analysis, Development & Support, Organisation, Language Use & Conventions; Score 2–12 (does not impact composite score)

faqs.question: Is the ACT Writing (Essay) section required for college admission?
faqs.answer: The Writing section is optional. However, some colleges and universities require or recommend it for admission or academic placement. Students should check each target school's specific requirements. When in doubt, it is generally advisable to complete the Writing section.

faqs.question: What is the average ACT score in the USA?
faqs.answer: The national average ACT composite score is approximately 19–20 out of 36. Competitive universities typically look for scores of 28–34+. For the most selective schools (top Ivy League, MIT, Stanford), the middle 50% of admitted students often score 34–36.

faqs.question: How many times can I take the ACT, and which score will colleges see?
faqs.answer: There is no official limit on ACT attempts. With ACT's Score Choice policy, students can choose which test dates' scores to send to colleges. Most colleges accept the Superscore (highest section scores across multiple test dates combined into one composite), making multiple attempts strategically advantageous.

faqs.question: What is the difference between the ACT and the SAT?
faqs.answer: Both are college admissions tests accepted by all US universities. Key differences: the ACT has a dedicated Science section; the SAT has a stronger reading/evidence-based focus. The ACT allows calculators throughout Math; the SAT has a no-calculator math section. Neither penalises for wrong answers. Most students perform comparably on both — taking a practice test for each is recommended to find your best fit.

meta.fullName: Concours d'entrée et d'accès aux études de premier cycle en sciences médicales et dentaires
meta.conductingBody: ARES — Académie de Recherche et d'Enseignement Supérieur (Fédération Wallonie-Bruxelles)
meta.description: Concours annuel centralisé organisé par l'ARES en Fédération Wallonie-Bruxelles pour l'accès aux études de médecine et de médecine dentaire. Il évalue les connaissances scientifiques (Biologie, Chimie, Physique, Mathématiques) et les compétences en communication et analyse critique.
meta.seoTitle: Concours Médecine Belgique 2026 : Tests Gratuits, Syllabus & Guide
meta.seoDescription: Préparez le concours d'entrée en médecine & dentisterie de Belgique (ARES) avec des tests gratuits & le syllabus 2026. Maîtrisez Bio, Chimie, Physique & Maths!

keyFacts.label: Fréquence
keyFacts.value: Deux sessions par an : session d'été (juillet/août) et session de janvier

keyFacts.label: Mode
keyFacts.value: Épreuve écrite sur papier dans des salles d'examen officielles

keyFacts.label: Durée totale
keyFacts.value: Environ 4 à 5 heures (Partie 1 + Partie 2)

keyFacts.label: Langue
keyFacts.value: Français uniquement

keyFacts.label: Validité du résultat
keyFacts.value: Le classement est valable pour l'année académique suivant la session

eligibility: Être titulaire ou être en cours d'obtention du Certificat d'Enseignement Secondaire Supérieur (CESS) ou d'un diplôme équivalent reconnu
Les candidats titulaires d'un diplôme étranger doivent obtenir une équivalence de diplôme auprès de la Fédération Wallonie-Bruxelles avant le jour du concours
L'inscription au concours doit être effectuée avant la date limite fixée chaque année par l'ARES via le portail officiel concoursmd.be
Il n'y a pas de limite d'âge ni de limite au nombre de tentatives autorisées

examPattern.label: Partie 1 — Épreuve de connaissances scientifiques
examPattern.value: 100 questions QCM : Biologie (50Q) | Chimie (20Q) | Physique (20Q) | Mathématiques (10Q)

examPattern.label: Partie 2 — Communication et analyse critique
examPattern.value: Questions portant sur la compréhension de textes, la synthèse, l'argumentation et le raisonnement clinique/éthique

examPattern.label: Format des questions
examPattern.value: QCM (Partie 1) et questions ouvertes/réponses courtes (Partie 2)

examPattern.label: Notation
examPattern.value: Classement final basé sur le score combiné des deux parties ; pas de note minimale fixe

examPattern.label: Marquage négatif
examPattern.value: Variable selon l'année — toujours vérifier les instructions officielles de l'ARES

syllabusOverview.subject: Biologie
syllabusOverview.topics: Biologie cellulaire et moléculaire (structure cellulaire, membrane plasmique, organites), ADN, réplication, transcription et traduction (dogme central), Génétique mendélienne et moléculaire (mutations, hérédité, groupes sanguins), Mitose et méiose, Métabolisme (glycolyse, cycle de Krebs, chaîne respiratoire, photosynthèse), Physiologie humaine (systèmes cardiovasculaire, respiratoire, digestif, rénal, nerveux, endocrinien, immunitaire), Microbiologie & virologie de base, Histologie et embryologie de base

syllabusOverview.subject: Chimie
syllabusOverview.topics: Structure de la matière (atomes, ions, tableau périodique, liaisons chimiques), Stœchiométrie et équilibres chimiques, Thermodynamique chimique (enthalpie, entropie, énergie libre de Gibbs), Cinétique chimique (vitesse de réaction, catalyse), Équilibres acido-basiques (pH, pKa, tampons), Chimie organique : fonctions chimiques (alcools, aldéhydes, cétones, acides carboxyliques, amines, esters), Nomenclature IUPAC, Réactivité et mécanismes réactionnels de base, Biomolécules (glucides, lipides, acides aminés, protéines)

syllabusOverview.subject: Physique
syllabusOverview.topics: Mécanique : cinématique (MRU, MRUA), dynamique (lois de Newton, frottements), travail, énergie et puissance, Mécanique des fluides (pression, poussée d'Archimède, équation de Bernoulli), Thermodynamique : lois de la thermodynamique, transferts thermiques, Électricité et magnétisme : circuits électriques (loi d'Ohm, Kirchhoff), champ électrique et magnétique, induction, Optique géométrique (réflexion, réfraction, lentilles, œil), Ondes : acoustique, lumière, spectre électromagnétique, Physique moderne : radioactivité, rayonnements ionisants, applications médicales

syllabusOverview.subject: Mathématiques
syllabusOverview.topics: Algèbre : équations et inéquations (1er et 2nd degré), systèmes d'équations, polynômes, Fonctions et analyse : limites, continuité, dérivation (règles, applications), intégration (calcul d'aires), Trigonométrie : fonctions trigonométriques, identités, équations, Géométrie analytique : droites, coniques (cercle, ellipse, parabole), vecteurs dans le plan, Statistiques et probabilités : statistique descriptive, probabilités (loi binomiale, normale), espérance et variance

syllabusOverview.subject: Communication et analyse critique
syllabusOverview.topics: Compréhension et synthèse de textes scientifiques et médicaux, Analyse d'arguments et détection de raisonnements fallacieux, Résolution de situations éthiques dans un contexte médical, Communication empathique et professionnelle, Raisonnement clinique de base et prise de décision, Structuration d'une argumentation écrite claire et cohérente

faqs.question: Combien de fois puis-je passer le concours par an ?
faqs.answer: Le concours est organisé deux fois par an : une session en été (généralement en juillet-août) et une session en janvier. Les candidats peuvent se présenter aux deux sessions la même année académique, leur meilleur résultat étant retenu.

faqs.question: Y a-t-il une note de passage minimale au concours ?
faqs.answer: Il n'existe pas de note de passage absolue fixe. Les candidats sont classés selon leur score total, et l'admission dépend du classement obtenu par rapport au nombre de places disponibles dans les facultés. Le classement varie donc d'une session à l'autre en fonction du niveau général des candidats.

faqs.question: Le concours est-il le même pour la médecine et la médecine dentaire ?
faqs.answer: Oui, en Fédération Wallonie-Bruxelles, le même concours d'entrée (organisé par l'ARES) donne accès aux études de médecine et de médecine dentaire. Les candidats indiquent leur choix de filière lors de l'inscription, et le classement est établi séparément pour chaque filière.

faqs.question: Comment se préparer efficacement au concours ?
faqs.answer: La préparation idéale combine une révision approfondie des mathématiques et sciences de la dernière année de l'enseignement secondaire (en particulier biologie, chimie et physique), des exercices sur les QCM de sessions antérieures disponibles sur concoursmd.be, et la pratique de textes d'analyse critique. Des cours préparatoires privés et les sessions de préparation organisées par certaines universités francophones belges sont également très utiles.

meta.description: The LSO Paralegal Licensing Examination (P1) is the mandatory competency-based assessment required to obtain a Class P1 licence to practise as an independent paralegal in Ontario, Canada. It tests the ability to apply law and professional judgment across ethics, civil litigation, criminal law, and practice management. The exam is open-book and conducted at designated Computer-Based Testing centres.
meta.seoTitle: LSO Paralegal Licensing Exam 2026: Fees, Dates, Format & How to Pass
meta.seoDescription: Complete guide to the 2026 LSO P1 Paralegal Licensing Exam. Confirmed fees ($1,460 + HST), exam dates, format, permitted materials, and a proven study strategy.

keyFacts.label: Frequency
keyFacts.value: 3 sittings per year (Summer: June, Autumn: October, Winter: January–February)

keyFacts.label: Mode
keyFacts.value: In-person Computer-Based Testing (CBT) at designated centres only — no remote or online proctoring

keyFacts.label: Duration
keyFacts.value: 7 hours total: two 3.5-hour sessions separated by a 30-minute break

keyFacts.label: Scoring
keyFacts.value: No fixed pass percentage; pass mark set per sitting using the modified Angoff method

keyFacts.label: Exam Fee (2026)
keyFacts.value: $1,460 + HST (increased from $1,075 in the previous cycle); application fee $160 + HST additional

keyFacts.label: Score Reporting
keyFacts.value: Subject-area performance feedback provided via LSO Connects portal after results are released

eligibility: Must be at least 18 years of age
Must have graduated from an LSO-accredited paralegal diploma programme (typically 1–2 years at an Ontario college)
Must satisfy the Good Character requirement under the Law Society Act, including disclosure of any prior criminal charges or findings

Internationally trained legal professionals are not exempt and must follow the standard paralegal licensing process; bridging pathway options are available via the Office of the Fairness Commissioner on the LSO website
There is no stated limit on the number of times a candidate may attempt the exam; subject-area feedback is provided to support repeat writers

examPattern.label: Professional Responsibility & Ethics
examPattern.value: High question volume | Tests: Paralegal Rules of Conduct, conflicts of interest, confidentiality, privilege, withdrawal from representation, client relations and retainers

examPattern.label: Practice Management & Trust Accounting
examPattern.value: High question volume | Tests: Trust account bookkeeping and calculations, client identification, file management, retainer administration, technology and data security

examPattern.label: Civil Litigation, Small Claims Court & Administrative Law
examPattern.value: Tests: Rules of the Small Claims Court, statutes of limitations, Statutory Powers Procedure Act (SPPA), tribunal practice at LTB, HRTO, and WSIB/WSIAT

examPattern.label: Criminal & Quasi-Criminal Law
examPattern.value: Tests: Provincial Offences Act (Parts I and III), Criminal Code summary conviction offences, Charter of Rights and Freedoms, sentencing principles and appeals

examPattern.label: Total Questions
examPattern.value: Approximately 160 multiple-choice questions (exact count not published in advance by the LSO)

examPattern.label: Negative Marking
examPattern.value: None — unanswered questions score zero; educated guessing is always advisable

examPattern.label: Permitted Materials
examPattern.value: Open-book: printed LSO-prepared study materials and a personal or third-party index (bound, e.g., in a 3-ring binder); no digital devices permitted

syllabusOverview.subject: Professional Responsibility & Ethics
syllabusOverview.topics: Paralegal Rules of Conduct and Guidelines in full; mandatory vs. discretionary obligations ('shall' vs. 'may'); professionalism, integrity, and civility; establishing and managing client retainers; identifying and resolving actual and potential conflicts of interest; solicitor-client privilege and confidentiality obligations; withdrawal from representation (mandatory and optional); duties to the tribunal and the public

syllabusOverview.subject: Practice Management & Trust Accounting
syllabusOverview.topics: Trust account rules and bookkeeping (including numerical calculations); general account vs. trust account distinctions; handling and recording client funds; client identification and verification requirements (FINTRAC obligations); file opening, management, and closing procedures; retainer agreements and billing practices; professional liability and errors-and-omissions obligations; technology use, data security, and cybersecurity obligations

syllabusOverview.subject: Civil Litigation, Small Claims Court & Administrative Law
syllabusOverview.topics: Small Claims Court Rules: pleadings (plaintiff's claim, defence, defendant's claim), motions, settlement conferences, trial procedure, and enforcement of orders; limitation periods under the Limitations Act 2002; Statutory Powers Procedure Act (SPPA): procedural fairness, notice, hearings, and tribunal decisions; Landlord and Tenant Board (LTB): Residential Tenancies Act, applications, hearings, and orders; Human Rights Tribunal of Ontario (HRTO): grounds of discrimination, application process; WSIB and WSIAT: appeals and benefit entitlements

syllabusOverview.subject: Criminal & Quasi-Criminal Law
syllabusOverview.topics: Provincial Offences Act — Part I (Certificate of Offence procedure), Part III (Information procedure), trials, appeals, and sentencing; Criminal Code of Canada: summary conviction offences within paralegal scope of practice, bail hearings, plea proceedings, sentencing submissions; Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms: ss. 7–14 rights (life, liberty, security; unreasonable search and seizure; arbitrary detention; right to counsel; right to silence; trial within reasonable time; right against self-incrimination); remedies under s. 24; sentencing principles and conditional discharges

syllabusOverview.subject: Cross-Cutting Application Skills
syllabusOverview.topics: Scenario-based application of law across all subject areas; identifying the legally correct course of action where multiple options appear plausible; reading exam questions strategically (question-first, then fact pattern); managing time across 160+ questions over a 7-hour sitting; navigating printed materials efficiently under time pressure

faqs.question: How much does the LSO Paralegal Licensing Exam cost in 2026?
faqs.answer: The exam fee for the 2026–2027 licensing year is $1,460 + HST, a significant increase from $1,075 in the previous cycle. An additional non-refundable application fee of $160 + HST applies. Always verify the current fee schedule on the LSO Connects portal before submitting payment.

faqs.question: Is the LSO P1 exam available online?
faqs.answer: No. As of 2026, the exam is conducted exclusively in person at designated Computer-Based Testing (CBT) centres in Ontario. Remote and online proctoring options are not offered for this examination.

faqs.question: What is the passing score for the LSO P1 exam?
faqs.answer: There is no fixed percentage pass mark. The LSO uses the modified Angoff method, where a standard-setting panel of practising paralegals reviews each sitting and sets the pass mark based on the assessed difficulty of that specific exam. This means a harder exam does not automatically produce a higher failure rate.

faqs.question: What materials can I bring into the P1 exam?
faqs.answer: The exam is open-book. Candidates may bring printed copies of the official LSO-prepared 2026 Paralegal Licensing Study Materials and a personal or third-party index (e.g., from Emond Publishing or PPA Prep), all bound in a 3-ring binder. No digital devices, phones, tablets, or unauthorised notes are permitted.

faqs.question: How many times can I sit the LSO Paralegal Licensing Exam?
faqs.answer: The LSO does not impose a stated limit on the number of attempts. After each unsuccessful attempt, candidates receive subject-area performance feedback through their LSO Connects portal to help target preparation for the next sitting.

faqs.question: Can internationally trained legal professionals apply for a paralegal licence in Ontario?
faqs.answer: Yes. Internationally trained candidates are not exempt from the P1 exam and must follow the standard paralegal licensing process. Bridging pathway options specific to international backgrounds are available through the Office of the Fairness Commissioner section of the LSO website.
